ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Bak Kut Teh (dry). FOODON_03311737 (pork rib dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Dry variant distinguished by reduced broth and concentrated sauce.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this pork-rib-dominant dish; no dedicated BKT code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated code for Bak Kut Teh. A0FKP (cooked pork) is the closest defensible anchor. Dry variant involves simmering with dark soy sauce and spices with minimal residual broth.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Bak Kut Teh or its dry variant as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of dry Bak Kut Teh is approximately 350 g, comprising pork ribs with a thick, dark soy-based sauce and minimal residual broth, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Foodo Database: Bak Kut Teh
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bak Kut Teh used as primary source. The dry variant has a reduced broth volume and higher sauce concentration compared to the soup variant; macronutrient values are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for pork rib dishes. Micronutrient values for potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, and vitamins are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork ribs (cooked, braised) — USDA NDB No. 10124 — to fill fields not explicitly reported by HPB. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork spare ribs (cooked, braised) at approximately 2.9 g per 100 g of dish as prepared.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on pork-dominant low-carbohydrate braised meat dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Kut Teh (dry) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised pork dishes. The dish contains only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from dark soy sauce and trace spice components. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (15 × 1.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 0.63, rounded to 0.6. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein-dominant meat dishes as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; approximately 1,330 mg per standard 350 g serving), contributed by dark soy sauce and seasoning salts used in the braising liquid.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 58% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and high protein content (9.5 g per 100 g) result in a low glycemic index (estimated 15) and negligible glycemic load per serving (0.6), supporting postprandial blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dry Bak Kut Teh is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice or dough fritters; the pork rib protein and fat content slow gastric emptying and minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (estimated 2.9 g per 100 g) from pork ribs combined with high sodium (380 mg per 100 g) raises concern for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (150 g or less), choose leaner rib cuts, discard visible fat, and avoid the high-sodium braising sauce to reduce combined saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Garlic and Chinese medicinal herbs (star anise, cinnamon, cloves) used in the braising spice blend contain bioactive compounds with reported anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dry Bak Kut Teh may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the herb and spice components may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit, though clinical evidence for the dish as prepared is limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh (Dry) is a Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese pork rib dish in which meaty pork ribs are slow-braised in a concentrated dark soy sauce enriched with garlic, white peppercorns, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and dried chillies. Unlike the soup (wet) variant, which features a clear, peppery broth, the dry version is characterised by a thick, glossy, intensely savoury sauce that coats the ribs with minimal residual liquid. The ribs are simmered for one to two hours until the meat is tender and falls easily from the bone. The dish delivers a bold umami flavour with a peppery, slightly sweet, and aromatic spice profile. Texturally, the meat is soft and succulent with a caramelised exterior from the dark soy reduction. Nutritionally, dry Bak Kut Teh is a high-protein (9.5 g per 100 g), moderate-fat (7.8 g per 100 g) dish with very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is 15 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 0.6. Sodium content is high at 380 mg per 100 g, equivalent to approximately 1,330 mg per serving, which is a primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of B vitamins, particularly thiamine (0.35 mg), niacin (3.2 mg), and vitamin B12 (0.45 mcg) per 100 g, reflecting the pork rib base. It is traditionally served with steamed white rice, you tiao (fried dough fritters), and preserved vegetables, and is consumed as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner dish across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo database is the primary approved source for this Singaporean dish. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from HPB with high confidence. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork ribs (cooked, braised) as HPB does not always report saturated fat breakdown for composite dishes. Micronutrients (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked pork ribs. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted source match for saturated fat and the analogous GI estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for dry Bak Kut Teh as a composite braised dish are not explicitly reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific preparation. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork spare ribs (cooked, braised), NDB No. 10124, was consulted but does not provide a directly applicable composite-dish fatty acid breakdown or moisture value for the dry BKT preparation as served (with sauce reduction and spice components). These five fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data not available from any approved source for this specific dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.9 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork spare ribs, cooked, braised (NDB No. 10124), adjusted for dish dilution with sauce and vegetables. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of high-protein, low-carbohydrate braised pork dishes; no direct GI measurement for Bak Kut Teh (dry) exists in the database as of 2026. The input record included 'plant_based' as a dietary tag, which has been removed as this dish is pork-based and not plant-based. Halal status is false as the dish contains pork. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100 g × serving_g) / 100 = (15 × 1.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 0.63, rounded to 0.6.
